question.text: In the depository system, what does 'fungibility' of securities mean?

answer.text: Securities in the same class are identical and interchangeable, without distinctive numbers
Securities can be converted from one type to another freely
Securities can be traded on any stock exchange without restriction
Securities can be pledged as collateral for loans

explanation.text: **The Logic:** In the depository system, dematerialised securities are not identified by distinctive numbers and certificate numbers. All securities in the same class are identical and interchangeable. **The Trap:** 'Securities can be traded on any stock exchange' describes portability/interoperability, not fungibility. Fungibility specifically refers to the interchangeability of identical units. **Cross-Exam Context:** Series VIII (Equity Derivatives) also uses the concept of fungibility in the context of standardised derivative contracts. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Example: All fully paid-up equity shares of Reliance are fungible — you cannot distinguish one share from another in the demat system. This is unlike physical certificates which had unique certificate numbers.
